title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome
oneLiner: Acute immune-mediated polyradiculoneuropathy causing ascending flaccid paralysis; can cause life-threatening respiratory and autonomic failure.

sections.Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS): **One-liner:** Acute immune-mediated polyradiculoneuropathy causing ascending flaccid paralysis; can cause life-threatening respiratory and autonomic failure.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Respiratory failure** — phrenic nerve/diaphragm weakness → hypoventilation  
  *Single finding:* Inability to count to 15 in one breath (or vital capacity <15–20 mL/kg).  
- **Dysautonomia** — labile BP, cardiac arrhythmias (brady/tachy), ileus  
  *Single finding:* Postural BP drop >30 mmHg + heart rate variability extremes.  
- **Mimic kill:** Acute spinal cord compression (trauma, myelopathy)  
  *Single finding:* Sensory level on exam (sharp/dull difference) — act on it with urgent MRI spine.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral ascending weakness (legs → arms) over days–weeks
  - Reduced/absent deep tendon reflexes (DTRs)
  - Distal paraesthesias/tingling (often in legs/fingers)
  - Back pain (common, especially in children)
  - Autonomic: constipation, urinary retention, sweating, BP lability
  - Cranial nerve: facial diplegia (50%), ophthalmoplegia (Miller Fisher variant), dysphagia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Symmetric weakness (proximal and distal)
  - Areﬂexia/hyporeﬂexia (even in strong limbs)
  - Sensory: mild proprioceptive/vibration loss (stocking distribution)
  - Miller Fisher: ataxia + areﬂexia + ophthalmoplegia (can overlap)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Spinal cord compression** — acute onset, sensory level, bladder/bowel involvement
- **Transverse myelitis** — sensory level, spinal MRI shows T2 hyperintensity
- **Myasthenia gravis** — fluctuating weakness, ocular onset, ptosis, no sensory loss
- **Botulism** — descending paralysis, bulbar early, dilated pupils, GI symptoms, history of home-preserved food
- **Tick paralysis** — ascending weakness + tick on scalp (remove it → recovery)
- **Polio / enterovirus** — asymmetric, fever, meningism, CSF pleocytosis
- **Acute intermittent porphyria** — abdominal pain, psychiatric features, urine porphobilinogen
- **Libyan disease (Canavan)** — very rare; consider in CSF with normal protein

**Splitter:** The presence of a *sensory level* (sharp/dull difference) on exam → spinal cord pathology, not GBS. Order urgent MRI spine.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Vital capacity (VC) using spirometer — if unavailable: single breath count (<20 = concern)
  - NEWS2, BP lying/standing, oxygen saturations
  - Cranial nerve exam (especially gag, cough)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR
  - HIV, VZV, EBV, CMV, Mycoplasma serology (identify trigger)
  - Anti-GM1/IgG antibodies (optional but helpful; positive in 30–60%)
  - Consider thyroid function, B12 (exclude mimics)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI spine (full or targeted) to exclude cord compression/transverse myelitis
  - Second-line: CT head (if cranial nerve involvement atypical)
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture (after MRI spine) — CSF protein elevated (1–10g/L) with normal WBC (<10) = albuminocytologic dissociation
  - Nerve conduction studies/EMG — after 2–3 weeks shows demyelinating pattern (prolonged distal latencies, conduction block)
- **Diagnostic criteria (Brighton Collaboration):**
  - Level 1: bilateral flaccid limb weakness + areflexia + LP with elevated CSF protein + NCS consistent with GBS
  - Level 2–4: fewer supporting features or limited resources
- **Never-miss test:** **Vital capacity measurement** (or single breath count) — omission leads to missed respiratory failure. Also **MRI spine** before LP to rule out cord compression.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess airway, breathing, circulation (ABC)
  - Measure VC (q4–6h) — if VC <20 mL/kg or rapidly falling → call ICU/anaesthetics
  - Admit to HDU/ICU if VC <20 mL/kg, bulbar weakness, or autonomic instability
  - No steroids — they worsen GBS outcomes
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Start **IVIg** (or plasma exchange) as soon as diagnosis is probable — do not wait for all tests
  - Monitor: VC q4–6h, NEWS2, BP lying/standing, heart rate, urine output
  - Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) prophylaxis — LMWH if no bleeding risk
  - Assess swallow — if dysphagia, NBM, consider NG tube
  - Neuropathic pain: gabapentin or pregabalin (start low, titrate)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Inpatient multi‑disciplinary rehabilitation (physio, OT, SLT)
  - Outpatient follow‑up with neurology
  - Consider home ventilator weaning plan if tracheostomy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride maintenance (avoid excessive IV fluids if autonomic instability)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (or local policy) — check bleeding risk
  - Diet: NBM if swallow unsafe (swallow assessment by SLT); otherwise soft diet with upright position
  - Observations: NEWS2 q1h (if unstable) → q4h when stable; VC q4–6h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line therapy:**
  - **IVIg (human immunoglobulin)** 0.4 g/kg/day IV for 5 days (total dose 2 g/kg) — use slow infusion per local protocol (e.g. start at 0.5 mL/kg/h, titrate up)
  - **Alternative: Plasma exchange** — 5 sessions over 7–10 days (requires specialist unit)
- **Pain management:**
  - Gabapentin: start 300mg PO once daily (renal adjusted) or pregabalin 75mg PO BD – titrate to effect
  - Avoid opioids unless breakthrough; constipation can worsen ileus
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - IVIg – caution in IgA deficiency (check IgA levels before; if low consider alternative)
  - Plasma exchange – avoid in sepsis, unstable haemodynamics
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - IVIg – dose adjustment not needed but monitor fluid balance in renal impairment
  - Gabapentin – reduce dose for eGFR <60 mL/min (e.g. if eGFR 30–59, start 300mg daily; <30, start 300mg alternate days)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - IVIg: too rapid infusion → anaphylaxis, aseptic meningitis, thromboembolism. Always start slow and follow local infusion rate tables.
  - Omission: starting IVIg late (>2 weeks) reduces efficacy; do not delay.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure (50% require ICU)
  - Dysautonomia: arrhythmias, labile BP, ileus, urinary retention
  - DVT / pulmonary embolism (immobility)
  - SIADH (Hyponatraemia)
  - Aspiration pneumonia
  - Neuropathic pain
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual weakness / fatigue (30–50% have long-term disability)
  - Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP) — if relapsing/remitting after 8 weeks
  - Psychosocial: depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress
sections.Prognosis: - 80–90% recover, but recovery often takes months to years; ~5% die (mostly from respiratory or autonomic complications). Poor prognostic factors: older age, rapid onset, need for ventilation, severe axonal involvement on NCS.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always measure VC on admission and q4–6h** — this is the single most important monitoring tool.
- **Start IVIg early** — within 2 weeks of symptom onset. Do not wait for LP or NCS results if clinical picture is clear.
- **Monitor for autonomic storms** — before, during, and after treatment. Have atropine and metoprolol drawn up if severe brady/tachy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT give steroids** — they worsen outcomes in GBS (unless treating CIDP later).
- **Do NOT delay treatment for confirmatory tests** — if Brighton level 1–2 criteria are met, start IVIg.
- **Do NOT miss spinal cord compression** — always do MRI spine if there is any sensory level or sphincter involvement.
- **Do NOT use opioids for pain without laxatives** — ileus in GBS + opiates = dangerous constipation.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** difficulty breathing, difficulty swallowing, worsening weakness in any limb, new slurred speech, or fainting spells.
- **Follow-up:** Neurology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks; if discharged on gabapentin/pregabalin, GP to monitor pain.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ICU/anaesthetics immediately if:**
  - VC <20 mL/kg or rapidly falling (>30% drop in 4h)
  - Single breath count <10
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any respiratory distress (tachypnoea, desaturation)
- **Call senior / neurology reg if:**
  - New bulbar weakness (dysphagia, weak cough)
  - Autonomic instability: HR <40 or >140, systolic BP <80 or >200
  - GCS <15 or confusion
- **Crash team** — for cardiac arrest or severe bradycardia leading to syncope (consider transcutaneous pacing if refractory).

rawMarkdown:
## Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS)
**One-liner:** Acute immune-mediated polyradiculoneuropathy causing ascending flaccid paralysis; can cause life-threatening respiratory and autonomic failure.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Respiratory failure** — phrenic nerve/diaphragm weakness → hypoventilation  
  *Single finding:* Inability to count to 15 in one breath (or vital capacity <15–20 mL/kg).  
- **Dysautonomia** — labile BP, cardiac arrhythmias (brady/tachy), ileus  
  *Single finding:* Postural BP drop >30 mmHg + heart rate variability extremes.  
- **Mimic kill:** Acute spinal cord compression (trauma, myelopathy)  
  *Single finding:* Sensory level on exam (sharp/dull difference) — act on it with urgent MRI spine.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral ascending weakness (legs → arms) over days–weeks
  - Reduced/absent deep tendon reflexes (DTRs)
  - Distal paraesthesias/tingling (often in legs/fingers)
  - Back pain (common, especially in children)
  - Autonomic: constipation, urinary retention, sweating, BP lability
  - Cranial nerve: facial diplegia (50%), ophthalmoplegia (Miller Fisher variant), dysphagia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Symmetric weakness (proximal and distal)
  - Areﬂexia/hyporeﬂexia (even in strong limbs)
  - Sensory: mild proprioceptive/vibration loss (stocking distribution)
  - Miller Fisher: ataxia + areﬂexia + ophthalmoplegia (can overlap)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Spinal cord compression** — acute onset, sensory level, bladder/bowel involvement
- **Transverse myelitis** — sensory level, spinal MRI shows T2 hyperintensity
- **Myasthenia gravis** — fluctuating weakness, ocular onset, ptosis, no sensory loss
- **Botulism** — descending paralysis, bulbar early, dilated pupils, GI symptoms, history of home-preserved food
- **Tick paralysis** — ascending weakness + tick on scalp (remove it → recovery)
- **Polio / enterovirus** — asymmetric, fever, meningism, CSF pleocytosis
- **Acute intermittent porphyria** — abdominal pain, psychiatric features, urine porphobilinogen
- **Libyan disease (Canavan)** — very rare; consider in CSF with normal protein

**Splitter:** The presence of a *sensory level* (sharp/dull difference) on exam → spinal cord pathology, not GBS. Order urgent MRI spine.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Vital capacity (VC) using spirometer — if unavailable: single breath count (<20 = concern)
  - NEWS2, BP lying/standing, oxygen saturations
  - Cranial nerve exam (especially gag, cough)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR
  - HIV, VZV, EBV, CMV, Mycoplasma serology (identify trigger)
  - Anti-GM1/IgG antibodies (optional but helpful; positive in 30–60%)
  - Consider thyroid function, B12 (exclude mimics)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI spine (full or targeted) to exclude cord compression/transverse myelitis
  - Second-line: CT head (if cranial nerve involvement atypical)
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture (after MRI spine) — CSF protein elevated (1–10g/L) with normal WBC (<10) = albuminocytologic dissociation
  - Nerve conduction studies/EMG — after 2–3 weeks shows demyelinating pattern (prolonged distal latencies, conduction block)
- **Diagnostic criteria (Brighton Collaboration):**
  - Level 1: bilateral flaccid limb weakness + areflexia + LP with elevated CSF protein + NCS consistent with GBS
  - Level 2–4: fewer supporting features or limited resources
- **Never-miss test:** **Vital capacity measurement** (or single breath count) — omission leads to missed respiratory failure. Also **MRI spine** before LP to rule out cord compression.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess airway, breathing, circulation (ABC)
  - Measure VC (q4–6h) — if VC <20 mL/kg or rapidly falling → call ICU/anaesthetics
  - Admit to HDU/ICU if VC <20 mL/kg, bulbar weakness, or autonomic instability
  - No steroids — they worsen GBS outcomes
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Start **IVIg** (or plasma exchange) as soon as diagnosis is probable — do not wait for all tests
  - Monitor: VC q4–6h, NEWS2, BP lying/standing, heart rate, urine output
  - Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) prophylaxis — LMWH if no bleeding risk
  - Assess swallow — if dysphagia, NBM, consider NG tube
  - Neuropathic pain: gabapentin or pregabalin (start low, titrate)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Inpatient multi‑disciplinary rehabilitation (physio, OT, SLT)
  - Outpatient follow‑up with neurology
  - Consider home ventilator weaning plan if tracheostomy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride maintenance (avoid excessive IV fluids if autonomic instability)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (or local policy) — check bleeding risk
  - Diet: NBM if swallow unsafe (swallow assessment by SLT); otherwise soft diet with upright position
  - Observations: NEWS2 q1h (if unstable) → q4h when stable; VC q4–6h

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line therapy:**
  - **IVIg (human immunoglobulin)** 0.4 g/kg/day IV for 5 days (total dose 2 g/kg) — use slow infusion per local protocol (e.g. start at 0.5 mL/kg/h, titrate up)
  - **Alternative: Plasma exchange** — 5 sessions over 7–10 days (requires specialist unit)
- **Pain management:**
  - Gabapentin: start 300mg PO once daily (renal adjusted) or pregabalin 75mg PO BD – titrate to effect
  - Avoid opioids unless breakthrough; constipation can worsen ileus
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - IVIg – caution in IgA deficiency (check IgA levels before; if low consider alternative)
  - Plasma exchange – avoid in sepsis, unstable haemodynamics
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - IVIg – dose adjustment not needed but monitor fluid balance in renal impairment
  - Gabapentin – reduce dose for eGFR <60 mL/min (e.g. if eGFR 30–59, start 300mg daily; <30, start 300mg alternate days)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - IVIg: too rapid infusion → anaphylaxis, aseptic meningitis, thromboembolism. Always start slow and follow local infusion rate tables.
  - Omission: starting IVIg late (>2 weeks) reduces efficacy; do not delay.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure (50% require ICU)
  - Dysautonomia: arrhythmias, labile BP, ileus, urinary retention
  - DVT / pulmonary embolism (immobility)
  - SIADH (Hyponatraemia)
  - Aspiration pneumonia
  - Neuropathic pain
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual weakness / fatigue (30–50% have long-term disability)
  - Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP) — if relapsing/remitting after 8 weeks
  - Psychosocial: depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress

### Prognosis
- 80–90% recover, but recovery often takes months to years; ~5% die (mostly from respiratory or autonomic complications). Poor prognostic factors: older age, rapid onset, need for ventilation, severe axonal involvement on NCS.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always measure VC on admission and q4–6h** — this is the single most important monitoring tool.
- **Start IVIg early** — within 2 weeks of symptom onset. Do not wait for LP or NCS results if clinical picture is clear.
- **Monitor for autonomic storms** — before, during, and after treatment. Have atropine and metoprolol drawn up if severe brady/tachy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT give steroids** — they worsen outcomes in GBS (unless treating CIDP later).
- **Do NOT delay treatment for confirmatory tests** — if Brighton level 1–2 criteria are met, start IVIg.
- **Do NOT miss spinal cord compression** — always do MRI spine if there is any sensory level or sphincter involvement.
- **Do NOT use opioids for pain without laxatives** — ileus in GBS + opiates = dangerous constipation.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** difficulty breathing, difficulty swallowing, worsening weakness in any limb, new slurred speech, or fainting spells.
- **Follow-up:** Neurology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks; if discharged on gabapentin/pregabalin, GP to monitor pain.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call ICU/anaesthetics immediately if:**
  - VC <20 mL/kg or rapidly falling (>30% drop in 4h)
  - Single breath count <10
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any respiratory distress (tachypnoea, desaturation)
- **Call senior / neurology reg if:**
  - New bulbar weakness (dysphagia, weak cough)
  - Autonomic instability: HR <40 or >140, systolic BP <80 or >200
  - GCS <15 or confusion
- **Crash team** — for cardiac arrest or severe bradycardia leading to syncope (consider transcutaneous pacing if refractory).
